responses.strategic_rationale: The selected levers address critical tensions in participant recruitment, data collection, ethics compliance, staffing, data dissemination, and tool development. By navigating the balance between operational efficiency and participant experience, the project can enhance its chances of meeting success criteria while mitigating risks associated with participant engagement and data integrity.

levers.name: Participant Recruitment Strategies
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls participant recruitment by choosing between direct outreach, referral partnerships, and community engagement initiatives.
levers.consequences: Enhancing recruitment through direct outreach may yield a higher volume of participants, but it risks oversaturation of the local population. Referral partnerships with neurologists could ensure a more targeted participant pool, yet may limit diversity. Community engagement initiatives can foster trust and awareness but require significant time investment to build relationships.
options: Implement a targeted outreach campaign to local sleep clinics and general practitioners to identify potential participants.
Establish referral partnerships with regional neurologists to streamline participant identification and ensure clinical relevance.
Conduct community engagement events to raise awareness about NREM parasomnias and encourage self-referrals from the public.
levers.review_lever: The core tension lies in balancing recruitment volume with participant quality; however, none of the options address the potential for participant fatigue from repeated outreach efforts.

levers.name: Data Collection Methodologies
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls data collection methodologies by choosing between enhanced monitoring, standard monitoring, and hybrid approaches.
levers.consequences: Opting for enhanced monitoring can capture more detailed data but may increase participant burden and operational costs. Standard monitoring is less intrusive but risks missing critical parasomnia events. Hybrid approaches can balance detail and participant comfort, yet may complicate data integration and analysis.
options: Adopt enhanced monitoring protocols that include full PSG on select nights to capture detailed physiological data.
Utilize standard monitoring techniques with dry-electrode EEG and contact-free sensors to minimize participant burden.
Implement a hybrid approach that combines enhanced and standard monitoring based on participant needs and event occurrence.
levers.review_lever: The tension between data richness and participant comfort is critical; however, none of the options consider the potential for data overload and analysis complexity.

levers.name: Ethics and Compliance Framework
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls the ethics and compliance framework by choosing between strict adherence to existing guidelines, adaptive compliance strategies, and proactive stakeholder engagement.
levers.consequences: Strict adherence to existing guidelines ensures regulatory compliance but may slow down the research process. Adaptive compliance strategies can enhance flexibility but risk ethical oversights. Proactive stakeholder engagement fosters transparency and trust but requires additional resources and time.
options: Maintain strict adherence to AASM and local ethical guidelines to ensure compliance and minimize risk.
Develop adaptive compliance strategies that allow for flexibility in response to emerging ethical considerations during the study.
Engage proactively with stakeholders, including participants and community members, to build trust and ensure ethical transparency.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between regulatory compliance and research agility; however, none of the options adequately address the potential for participant concerns about privacy and data use.

levers.name: Operational Staffing Models
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls operational staffing models by choosing between full-time staff, part-time staff, and a mixed staffing approach.
levers.consequences: Utilizing full-time staff ensures consistency and reliability in participant care but increases fixed costs. Part-time staff can reduce costs but may lead to variability in participant experience. A mixed staffing approach can optimize resource allocation but complicates scheduling and training.
options: Employ full-time staff for all operational roles to ensure consistent participant care and data collection.
Utilize part-time staff for non-critical roles to reduce operational costs while maintaining core team stability.
Implement a mixed staffing model that combines full-time and part-time roles to balance cost and operational efficiency.
levers.review_lever: The tension between cost management and staffing reliability is significant; however, none of the options consider the potential impact of staff turnover on participant experience and data quality.

levers.name: Data Sharing and Publication Policies
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls data sharing and publication policies by choosing between open data sharing, restricted data sharing, and selective publication strategies.
levers.consequences: Open data sharing enhances transparency and collaboration but may raise privacy concerns among participants. Restricted data sharing protects participant confidentiality but limits broader scientific engagement. Selective publication strategies can maximize impact but risk perceptions of bias in reporting.
options: Adopt an open data sharing policy for de-identified physiological data to promote transparency and collaboration in research.
Implement a restricted data sharing policy that limits access to sensitive data while still allowing for some collaborative efforts.
Utilize selective publication strategies to highlight significant findings while managing the narrative around the research outcomes.
levers.review_lever: The core tension is between transparency and participant privacy; however, none of the options adequately address the potential for misinterpretation of shared data by external parties.

levers.name: Event-Triage Tool Development
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls the development of the event-triage tool by choosing between manual scoring, semi-automated scoring, and fully automated scoring systems.
levers.consequences: Manual scoring ensures high accuracy but is labor-intensive and may not scale effectively. Semi-automated scoring can reduce workload while maintaining reliability, yet may introduce variability in scoring. Fully automated scoring could streamline processes but risks significant errors without robust validation.
options: Develop a manual scoring system that relies on expert raters to ensure high accuracy in event adjudication.
Create a semi-automated scoring tool that assists raters by flagging potential events for review, balancing efficiency and accuracy.
Pursue a fully automated scoring system that utilizes advanced algorithms to classify events, aiming for scalability and speed.
levers.review_lever: The tension between accuracy and efficiency is critical; however, none of the options consider the potential for algorithmic bias in automated scoring systems.

levers.name: Participant Engagement Strategies
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls participant retention by choosing between active engagement initiatives, passive follow-up methods, and incentive-based retention strategies.
levers.consequences: Implementing active engagement initiatives can enhance participant satisfaction and retention rates, fostering a sense of community. However, this approach may require additional resources and time, potentially straining the budget and operational capacity.
options: Develop regular feedback sessions to gather participant insights and adapt the study environment accordingly.
Create a structured communication plan that includes updates and educational content about the study's progress and findings.
Introduce a tiered incentive program that rewards participants for continued involvement and completion of study milestones.
levers.review_lever: Balancing participant engagement with operational efficiency is crucial, but none of the options address the potential for participant fatigue from constant communication.

levers.name: Data Annotation Protocols
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls the quality of data analysis by choosing between manual scoring, semi-automated scoring, and fully automated scoring methods.
levers.consequences: Opting for manual scoring ensures high accuracy but is labor-intensive and may lead to delays in data processing. Conversely, fully automated scoring could expedite analysis but risks compromising data integrity if not properly validated.
options: Implement a dual scoring system where independent raters validate each other's assessments to enhance reliability.
Utilize a semi-automated scoring tool that assists raters by flagging potential events for review, balancing speed and accuracy.
Adopt a fully automated scoring system that leverages advanced algorithms to analyze data, contingent on thorough pre-validation against human scoring.
levers.review_lever: The tension between speed and accuracy is evident, but none of the options consider the potential learning curve and training time required for staff to adapt to new scoring systems.

levers.name: Facility Safety Enhancements
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls participant safety by choosing between standard safety measures, enhanced monitoring systems, and participant training programs.
levers.consequences: Enhancing monitoring systems can significantly improve participant safety and reduce incident rates, but it may also increase operational costs and complexity. Conversely, relying solely on standard safety measures might not adequately address unique risks associated with parasomnia events.
options: Install advanced monitoring systems that provide real-time alerts for unusual participant behavior, ensuring immediate response capabilities.
Conduct comprehensive safety training sessions for all staff to prepare them for potential emergencies related to parasomnia events.
Implement a buddy system where participants are paired for mutual support during their stay, fostering a safer environment.
levers.review_lever: While improving safety is paramount, the options overlook the potential psychological impact on participants of increased surveillance and monitoring.

levers.name: Data Sharing Framework
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls the accessibility of research data by choosing between restricted access, tiered access based on collaboration, and open access for de-identified data.
levers.consequences: Adopting an open access model for de-identified data can enhance the study's visibility and foster collaboration, but it may raise concerns about data privacy and misuse. Conversely, restricted access could protect sensitive information but limit the study's impact and reach.
options: Establish a tiered access system that allows collaborators to access specific datasets while maintaining strict privacy controls.
Create a public repository for de-identified data that includes clear guidelines on usage and citation to promote responsible sharing.
Limit data sharing to internal stakeholders and select collaborators, ensuring that sensitive information remains protected.
levers.review_lever: The challenge of balancing data accessibility with privacy concerns is critical, yet none of the options adequately address the potential for misuse of shared data.

levers.name: Research Collaboration Models
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls the structure of research partnerships by choosing between formal collaborations, informal networks, and industry partnerships.
levers.consequences: Formal collaborations can provide clear roles and responsibilities, enhancing accountability, but may also introduce bureaucratic delays. Informal networks can foster innovation but lack the structure needed for large-scale projects, while industry partnerships can offer funding but may impose constraints on research direction.
options: Establish formal collaborations with academic institutions to leverage expertise and resources while ensuring clear governance.
Create informal networks with other researchers to share insights and foster innovation without the constraints of formal agreements.
Pursue industry partnerships that align with research goals, ensuring that academic integrity and publication rights are preserved.
levers.review_lever: The tension between collaboration and independence is significant, but none of the options consider the potential conflicts of interest that may arise from industry partnerships.

levers.name: Participant Retention Strategies
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls participant retention by choosing between enhanced engagement activities, financial incentives, and streamlined communication protocols.
levers.consequences: Implementing enhanced engagement activities can significantly improve participant retention rates, fostering a sense of community and commitment. However, this may increase operational complexity and require additional resources for planning and execution.
options: Develop a series of social events and workshops to foster community among participants and encourage ongoing involvement.
Offer tiered financial incentives based on the length of participation and number of completed assessments to motivate continued engagement.
Establish a dedicated communication team to provide regular updates and personalized check-ins, ensuring participants feel valued and informed.
levers.review_lever: The core tension lies in balancing participant engagement with operational efficiency; however, none of the options address the potential burnout from excessive social activities.

levers.name: Data Annotation Efficiency
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls data annotation efficiency by choosing between manual scoring, semi-automated tools, and crowd-sourced annotation.
levers.consequences: Opting for semi-automated tools can streamline the annotation process, reducing the time required for data processing. However, reliance on technology may introduce errors if the tools are not rigorously validated against human scoring.
options: Utilize a manual scoring system with trained experts to ensure high accuracy in data annotation, albeit at a slower pace.
Implement semi-automated tools that assist human annotators by flagging potential events for review, balancing speed and accuracy.
Crowd-source the annotation process by training a larger group of volunteers to annotate data, increasing throughput but potentially compromising consistency.
levers.review_lever: The tension between speed and accuracy is critical; however, none of the options adequately consider the training and oversight required for crowd-sourced annotation.

levers.name: Facility Safety Protocols
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls facility safety protocols by choosing between enhanced monitoring systems, staff training programs, and participant safety audits.
levers.consequences: Enhancing monitoring systems can significantly improve participant safety during overnight stays, but it may also increase operational costs and complexity. Additionally, staff training programs require time and resources that could impact staffing availability.
options: Install advanced monitoring systems that provide real-time alerts for unusual participant behavior, ensuring immediate response capabilities.
Implement comprehensive staff training programs focused on emergency response and participant safety, enhancing overall care quality.
Conduct regular participant safety audits to identify potential risks and improve safety measures, though this may divert resources from other operational areas.
levers.review_lever: The challenge of ensuring participant safety while managing operational costs is evident; however, none of the options address the potential for staff fatigue from increased training demands.

levers.name: Research Collaboration Framework
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls the research collaboration framework by choosing between formal partnerships, informal networks, and interdisciplinary teams.
levers.consequences: Establishing formal partnerships can provide access to additional resources and expertise, but may also impose rigid structures that limit flexibility. Informal networks can foster creativity but may lack accountability.
options: Create formal partnerships with established research institutions to leverage their expertise and resources for enhanced study outcomes.
Develop informal networks among researchers to encourage knowledge sharing and innovative approaches, albeit with less structure.
Form interdisciplinary teams that integrate diverse expertise, promoting holistic research approaches but potentially complicating decision-making processes.
levers.review_lever: The tension between structure and flexibility in collaboration is crucial; however, none of the options consider the potential for conflicting priorities among diverse team members.

levers.name: Participant Recruitment Channels
levers.decision_axis: This lever controls participant recruitment channels by choosing between direct outreach, referral networks, and community engagement initiatives.
levers.consequences: Direct outreach can yield immediate results in participant recruitment, but may not sustain long-term engagement. Referral networks can provide a steady stream of qualified participants but may limit diversity in the sample.
options: Conduct direct outreach campaigns targeting local sleep clinics and neurologists to quickly identify potential participants for the study.
Leverage existing referral networks within the medical community to recruit participants who meet the study criteria, ensuring a more qualified sample.
Implement community engagement initiatives that raise awareness about parasomnias, attracting a broader range of participants and fostering public interest.
levers.review_lever: The challenge of balancing speed and diversity in recruitment is significant; however, none of the options address the potential for participant fatigue from repeated outreach efforts.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Classification**
   - `lever_type`: classify each lever as one of: methodology, execution, governance, dissemination, product, operations.
   - `decision_axis`: write one sentence describing the controllable choice. Use the template: "This lever controls X by choosing between A, B, and C." The axis must be a single, crisp decision — not a topic or workstream.

3. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

4. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

5. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

6. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

7. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
